{{Short description|None}}
{{Unreliable sources|date=January 2023}}
{{GeoGroup}}
This list includes potential but unconfirmed structures that are not listed on the [[Earth Impact Database]] list of confirmed impact structures. For confirmed impact structures, see [[List of impact structures on Earth]].

== List of confirmed and possible impact structures ==
The following tables list geological features on Earth that are possible impact events, but for which there is currently no confirming scientific evidence in the peer-reviewed literature. In order for a structure to be [[List of impact craters on Earth|confirmed]] as an impact crater, it must meet a stringent set of well-established criteria. Some proposed impact structures are likely to eventually be confirmed, whereas others are likely to be shown to have been misidentified (see below). Recent extensive surveys have been done for Australian (2005),<ref name=Haines>{{cite journal |doi=10.1080/08120090500170351  |url=https://www.researchgate.net/publication/236737663|title=Impact cratering and distal ejecta: The Australian record|year=2005|last1=Haines|first1=P. W.|journal=Australian Journal of Earth Sciences|volume=52|issue=4–5|pages=481–507|bibcode=2005AuJES..52..481H|s2cid=128705406}}</ref> African (2014),<ref name=Africa>{{Cite journal | doi=10.1016/j.jafrearsci.2014.01.008| title=Impact structures in Africa: A review| year=2014| last1=Reimold| first1=Wolf Uwe| last2=Koeberl| first2=Christian| journal=Journal of African Earth Sciences| volume=93| pages=57–175| pmid=27065753| pmc=4802546| bibcode=2014JAfES..93...57R}}</ref> and South American (2015)<ref>{{cite book |last1=Acevedo |first1=R. |last2=Rocca |first2=M. C. |last3=Ponce |first3=J. |last4=Stinco |first4=S. |title=Impact Craters in South America |date=2015 |publisher=Springer |series=SpringerBriefs in Earth Sciences |isbn=978-3-319-13092-7}}</ref> craters, as well as those in the Arab world (2016).<ref>{{cite book |first=M. C. |last=Chabou |year=2016 |chapter-url=https://www.researchgate.net/publication/304035970 |chapter=An updated inventory of meteorite impact structures in the Arab world |title=1st ArabGU International Conference, Feb 2016, Algeria}}</ref> A book review by A. Crósta and U. Reimold disputes some of the evidence presented for several of the South American structures.<ref>{{Cite journal |doi = 10.1111/maps.12632|title = Impact Craters in South America, by Acevedo R. D., Rocca M. C. L., Ponce J. F., and Stinco S. G. Heidelberg: Springer, 2015. 104 p. SpringerBriefs in Earth Sciences: South America and the Southern Hemisphere. {{text|ISBN}} 978-3-319-13092-7|year = 2016|last1 = Crósta|first1 = Alvaro P.|last2 = Reimold|first2 = Wolf Uwe|journal = Meteoritics & Planetary Science|volume = 51|issue = 5|pages = 996–999|doi-access = free}}</ref>

! Name
! Location
!Country
! Diameter (km)
! data-sort-type=number| Age ([[megaannum|Ma]])
!Confirmed
! class="unsortable" | Notes
! class=unsortable | Image
! Coordinates
|-
| [[38th parallel structures|38th Parallel structures]] 
| [[Missouri]], etc.

| {{sort|9.5|2-17}}
| {{sort|320|320 ± 10}}
|2/7 confirmed
| align="center" | <ref>{{cite journal |last1=Rampino |first1=M.R |last2=Volk |first2=T. |year=1996 |title=Multiple impact event in the Paleozoic: Collision with a string of comets or asteroids? |url=https://pubs.giss.nasa.gov/docs/1996/1996_Rampino_ra05200y.pdf |journal=[[Geophysical Research Letters]] |volume=23 |issue=1 |pages=49–52 |doi=10.1029/95GL03605 |bibcode=1996GeoRL..23...49R |access-date=2019-04-06}}</ref>

| align="center" | <ref>[http://labmpg.sscc.ru/a1142.html Charity Shoal]</ref><ref name="HolcombeOthers2001a">{{cite journal | doi=10.1016/S0380-1330(01)70664-8 | title=Small Rimmed Depression in Lake Ontario: An Impact Crater? | year=2001 | last1=Holcombe | first1=Troy L. | last2=Warren | first2=John S. | last3=Reid | first3=David F. | last4=Virden | first4=William T. | last5=Divins | first5=David L. | journal=Journal of Great Lakes Research | volume=27 | issue=4 | pages=510–517 | bibcode=2001JGLR...27..510H }}</ref><ref name="HolcombeOthers2013a">{{cite journal | doi=10.1007/s00367-013-0322-6 | title=Geological structure of Charity Shoal crater, Lake Ontario, revealed by multibeam bathymetry | year=2013 | last1=Holcombe | first1=Troy L. | last2=Youngblut | first2=Scott | last3=Slowey | first3=Niall | journal=Geo-Marine Letters | volume=33 | issue=4 | pages=245–252 | bibcode=2013GML....33..245H | s2cid=129846298 }}</ref><ref name="Suttak2013a">Suttak, P.A., 2013, ''High-resolution lake-based magnetic mapping and modeling of basement structures, with examples from Küçükçekmece Lagoon, Turkey and Charity Shoal, Lake Ontario.'' unpublished MS thesis, School of Geography and Earth Sciences, McMaster University, Hamilton, Ontario. 113 pp.</ref>
| [[File:NOAA_map_of_Charity_Shoal_in_Lake_Ontario.jpg|center|150px]]
| align="right" | {{Coord|44|2|15|N|76|29|37|W|name=Charity Shoal}}
|-

|Canada
| 4
| {{sort|469|<470}}
|
| align="center" | <ref>[http://labmpg.sscc.ru/a1423.html Corossol]</ref><ref name="HigginsOthers2013">Higgins, M.D., P. Lajeunesse, G. St-Onge, R. Sanfacon, and M. Duchesne, 2013, [http://www.hou.usra.edu/meetings/metsoc2013/pdf/5190.pdf ''Impact Breccia Clast from the Corossol Crater, Canada.''] 76th Annual Meteoritical Society Meeting. Meteoritics and Planetary Science Supplement. id.5190.</ref><ref name="LajeunesseOthers2013a">{{cite journal | doi=10.1111/maps.12224 | title=The Corossol structure: A possible impact crater on the seafloor of the northwestern Gulf of St. Lawrence, Eastern Canada | year=2013 | last1=Lajeunesse | first1=Patrick | last2=St-Onge | first2=Guillaume | last3=Locat | first3=Jacques | last4=Duchesne | first4=Mathieu J. | last5=Higgins | first5=Michael D. | last6=Sanfaçon | first6=Richard | last7=Ortiz | first7=Joseph | journal=Meteoritics & Planetary Science | volume=48 | issue=12 | pages=2542–2558 | bibcode=2013M&PS...48.2542L | s2cid=140639070 }}</ref><ref name="LajeunesseOthers2016a">Lajeunesse, P., Duchesne, M.J., St-Onge, G., Locat, J., Higgins, M., Sanfaçon, R. and Ortiz, J., 2016. ''The Corossol Structure: a glaciated crater of possible impact origin in the northwestern Gulf of St Lawrence, eastern Canada.'' In Dowdeswell, J. A., Canals, M., Jakobsson, M., Todd, B. J., Dowdeswell, E. K. & Hogan, K. A. (eds) 2016. ''Atlas of Submarine Glacial Landforms: Modern, Quaternary and Ancient.'' Geological Society, London, Memoirs, 46(1), pp.127–128.</ref>

| 66 ± 0.8
|

| [[Pilbara craton|Pilbara Craton]], [[Western Australia]]

| 3,470
|
| align="center" | <ref name="Kirkland2025">{{cite journal|last1=Kirkland |first1=C.L. |last2=Johnson |first2=T.E. |last3=Kaempf |first3=J. |last4=Ribeiro |first4=B.V. |last5=Zametzer |first5=A. |last6=Hugh Smithies |first6=R. |last7=McDonald |first7=B. |title=A Paleoarchaean impact crater in the Pilbara Craton, Western Australia |journal=[[Nature Communications]] |volume=16 |article-number=2224 (2025) |date=6 March 2025 |issue=1 |doi=10.1038/s41467-025-57558-3 |doi-access=free |pmid=40050265 |pmc=11885519 |bibcode=2025NatCo..16.2224K }}</ref><ref>de Kruijff, P. (6 March 2025). [https://www.abc.net.au/news/science/2025-03-06/oldest-known-crater-pilbara-geology-3-47-billion-years-old/105019606 Earth's oldest-known crater discovered in outback WA is 3.47 billion years old, study finds] ''[[ABC News (Australia)|ABC News]]''. Retrieved 6 March 2025.</ref>
|

Pt 1, Houston, Tex., pp. 77–78</ref><ref>{{cite journal | bibcode=1996LPI....27..725L | title=The Azuara and Rubielos Structures, Spain: Twin Impact Craters or Alpine Thrust Systems? TEM Investigations on Deformed Quartz Disprove Shock Origin | last1=Langenhorst | first1=F. | last2=Deutsch | first2=A. | journal=Lunar and Planetary Science Conference | year=1996 | volume=27 | page=725 }}</ref>{{refn|group=note|name=EIDunconfirmed}}

Pt 1., Houston (Tex.), p. 157</ref><ref>{{cite journal | doi=10.1016/0012-821X(95)00051-D | title=Magnesioferrite spinel in Cretaceous/Tertiary boundary sediments of the Pacific basin: Remnants of hot, early ejecta from the Chicxulub impact? | year=1995 | last1=Kyte | first1=Frank T. | last2=Bostwick | first2=Jennifer A. | journal=Earth and Planetary Science Letters | volume=132 | issue=1–4 | pages=113–127 | bibcode=1995E&PSL.132..113K }}</ref><ref>Kyte Frank T. | align="center" | <ref>[http://labmpg.sscc.ru/a168.html Silverpit]</ref><ref>{{cite journal |vauthors=Stewart SA, Allen PJ |title=A 20-km-diameter multi-ringed impact structure in the North Sea|journal=[[Nature (journal)|Nature]] | volume=418 | issue=6897 | year=2002 | pages=520–523 | pmid=12152076|doi=10.1038/nature00914|bibcode = 2002Natur.418..520S |s2cid=4381323}}</ref><ref>Collins G.S., Turtle E.P., Melosh H.J. (2003). Numerical simulations of silverpit crater collapse: a comparison of Tekton and SALES . LPI Contrib., No. 1155, p. 18</ref><ref>Stratford R. (2004). Bombarded Britain: A Search for British Impact Structures // Imperial College Press, London</ref><ref>Collins G., Pain C. C., Wilson C. (2006) MODELLING IMPACT CRATER COLLAPSE IN THREE DIMENSIONS</ref><ref>Conway Z.K., Haszeldine S., Rider M. (2006). Determining the origin of the Silverpit crater, UK southern North Sea: can you prove the existence of a meteorite crater without geochemical or mineralogical data?. 40 ESLAB Symposium: 1 International Conference on Impact Cratering in the Solar System, Noordwijk, 8–12 May 2006, Noordwijk: ESA, P. 53</ref><ref>Cartwright J., Davies R., Stewart S., Wall M. (2006) BURIAL OF THE SILVERPIT METEORITE CRATER</ref>

''Science''], 201(4352), pp. 279–279.</ref><ref name="PotterOthers2005a">Rajmon, D., 2012. [http://www.datapages.com/gis-map-publishing-program/gis-open-files/geothematic/david-rajmon-global-impact-crater-gis-project ''David Rajmon Global Impact Crater GIS Project''] AAPG Datapages, Tulsa Oklahoma: American Association of Petroleum Geologists.</ref>

| align="center" | <ref>[http://labmpg.sscc.ru/a1112.html Tatarsky South]</ref><ref name="Tatarsky" />
|
| align="right" | {{coord|48|17|38|N|141|23|40|E|name=Tatarsky2}}
|-

| {{sort|65|65 ± 20}}

[http://earthdoc.eage.org/publication/publicationdetails/?publication=47638 Geophysical Evidence Of A Possible Impact Structure At The K-T Boundary Of The Solimões Basin, Brazil] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20160808004712/http://earthdoc.eage.org/publication/publicationdetails/?publication=47638 |date=2016-08-08 }}, 6th International Congress of the Brazilian Geophysical Society</ref>

|United States

| 35
|
| align="center" | <ref>[http://labmpg.sscc.ru/a359.html Toms Canyon]</ref><ref>Glass B.P. (1987). Coesite associated with North American tektite debris in DSDP site 612 on the continental slope off NEW Jersey . Lunar and Planet. Sci. Houston (Tex.), s.a..  Vol. 18: 18th Conf., Houston Tex., March 16–20, 1987: Abstr. Pap., pp. 328–329</ref><ref>{{cite journal | doi=10.1016/S0025-3227(97)00113-8 | title=The Toms Canyon structure, New Jersey outer continental shelf: A possible late Eocene impact crater | year=1998 | last1=Poag | first1=C.Wylie | last2=Poppe | first2=Lawrence J. | journal=Marine Geology | volume=145 | issue=1–2 | pages=23–60 | bibcode=1998MGeol.145...23P }}</ref><ref>{{cite journal | doi=10.2110/jsr.2011.42 | title=Glauconite Composition and Morphology, Shocked Quartz, and the Origin of the Cretaceous(?) Main Fossiliferous Layer (MFL) in Southern New Jersey, U.S.A. | date=2011 | last1=Obasi | first1=C. C. | last2=Terry | first2=D. O. | last3=Myer | first3=G. H. | last4=Grandstaff | first4=D. E. | journal=Journal of Sedimentary Research | volume=81 | issue=7 | pages=479–494 | bibcode=2011JSedR..81..479O }}</ref>
|
| align="right" | {{coord|39|08|N|72|51|W|name=Toms Canyon}}
|-

| align="center" | <ref name="GliksonOthers2015a">{{cite journal | doi=10.1016/j.tecto.2014.12.010 | title=Geophysical anomalies and quartz deformation of the Warburton West structure, central Australia | year=2015 | last1=Glikson | first1=A.Y. | last2=Meixner | first2=A.J. | last3=Radke | first3=B. | last4=Uysal | first4=I.T. | last5=Saygin | first5=E. | last6=Vickers | first6=J. | last7=Mernagh | first7=T.P. | journal=Tectonophysics | volume=643 | pages=55–72 }}</ref><ref name="GliksonOthers2018a">Glikson, A.Y. and Pirajno, F., 2018. ''The World's Largest Late to Post-Archaean Asteroid Impact Structures''. In ''Asteroids Impacts, Crustal Evolution and Related Mineral Systems with Special Reference to Australia'' (pp. 61-78). Springer, Cham.,</ref><ref name="Glikson2018a">{{cite journal | doi=10.1016/j.tecto.2017.11.003 | title=Structure and origin of Australian ring and dome features with reference to the search for asteroid impact events | year=2018 | last1=Glikson | first1=Andrew | journal=Tectonophysics | volume=722 | pages=175–196 | bibcode=2018Tectp.722..175G | hdl=1885/139145 | hdl-access=free }}</ref>
|
|

In ''The sedimentary record of meteorite impacts.'' (pp. 55-64). ''Geological Society of America Special Paper'' No. 437.</ref><ref name="EvansOthers2003a">Evans, K.R., Mickus, K.L., Rovey II, C.W. and Davis, G.H., 2003. [http://www.missourigeologists.org/Meeting2003/RI-75(2003AMGguidebook).pdf#page=7 ''Field Trip I: The Weaubleau-Osceola Structure: Evidence of a Mississippian Meteorite Impact in Southwestern Missouri.''] [http://www.missourigeologists.org/FieldtripsandGuidebooks.htm Association of Missouri Geologists Field Trip Guidebook.] 50th Annual Meeting Springfield, Missouri September 26–27, 2003, Missouri Department of Natural Resources, Rollo, MIssouri.</ref><ref name="Beauford2015a">Beauford, R.E., 2015. [https://scholarworks.uark.edu/etd/1039/ ''Physical records of impacts in the early and modern solar system.''] PhD thesis, University of Arkansas, Fayetteville, Arkansas, 174 p.</ref>
| [[File:Weaubleau Structure shaded relief.jpg|center|150px]]

Congo): A Possible Impact Crater in Central Africa |author=G. Monegato |author2=M. Massironi |author3=E. Martellato |name-list-style=amp |bibcode=2010LPI....41.1601M }}</ref><ref>{{cite news| url=https://news.bbc.co.uk/2/hi/science/nature/8526093.stm | work=BBC News | title=Ring may be giant 'impact crater' | date=2010-03-10 | access-date=2010-05-08}}</ref>

|
| align="center" | <ref>[http://labmpg.sscc.ru/a870.html Woodbury]</ref><ref>E. F. Albin and R. S. Harris (2016). [http://www.hou.usra.edu/meetings/lpsc2016/pdf/1398.pdf WOODBURY ASTROBLEME: FURTHER EVIDENCE FOR A LATE PROTEROZOIC IMPACT STRUCTURE IN WEST-CENTRAL GEORGIA, US], 47th Lunar and Planetary Science Conference</ref><ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Halford |first1=Natalie |last2=Barineau |first2=Clinton |year=2019 |title=The Search for Impact Features in Proposed Target Rocks of the "Woodbury" Structure in the Pine Mountain Belt of Southwestern Georgia |url=https://gsa.confex.com/gsa/2019SE/webprogram/Paper327565.html |journal=Geological Society of America Abstracts with Programs |volume=51 |issue=3 |article-number=327565 |doi=10.1130/abs/2019SE-327565|bibcode=2019GSAA...5127565H |s2cid=195549826 |url-access=subscription }}</ref><ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Miller |first1=Jeremy |last2=Barineau |first2=Clinton |year=2016 |title=A Case of Mistaken Identity: The "Woodbury" Structure of South Central Georgia |url=https://gsa.confex.com/gsa/2016AM/webprogram/Paper285522.html |journal=Geological Society of America Abstracts with Programs |volume=48 |issue=7 |doi=10.1130/abs/2016AM-285522|url-access=subscription }}</ref>

| [[Yallalie]]

| align="center" | <ref>[http://labmpg.sscc.ru/a221.html Yallalie]</ref><ref name="tsun" /><ref>{{cite journal | last1 = Dentith | first1 = M. | last2 = Bevan | first2 = A. | last3 = Backhouse | first3 = J. | last4 = Featherstone | first4 = W. | last5 = Koeberl | first5 = C. | year = 1999 | title = Yallalie: a Buried Structure of Possible Impact Origin in the Perth Basin, Western Australia | url = http://espace.library.curtin.edu.au/R/?func=dbin-jump-full&object_id=146448&local_base=GEN01-ERA02 | journal = Geological Magazine | volume = 136 | issue = 6| pages = 619–632 | doi = 10.1017/s0016756899003386 | bibcode = 1999GeoM..136..619D | hdl = 20.500.11937/10289 | s2cid = 38389086 | hdl-access = free }}</ref><ref>Grant, B. [http://members.iinet.net.au/~gboxer/4_Yallalie.html The Yallalie Impact Structure].</ref><ref>{{cite journal | doi=10.1016/S0031-0182(00)00244-3 | title=An Upper Pliocene lacustrine environmental record from south-Western Australia — preliminary results | year=2001 | last1=Dodson | first1=J.R. | last2=Ramrath | first2=A. | journal=Palaeogeography, Palaeoclimatology, Palaeoecology | volume=167 | issue=3–4 | pages=309–320 | bibcode=2001PPP...167..309D }}</ref><ref>{{cite journal | doi=10.1016/j.gloplacha.2004.01.013 | title=Palynological evidence for aridity events and vegetation change during the Middle Pliocene, a warm period in Southwestern Australia | year=2004 | last1=Dodson | first1=J. | last2=MacPhail | first2=M. K. | journal=Global and Planetary Change | volume=41 | issue=3–4 | pages=285–307 | bibcode=2004GPC....41..285D }}</ref>{{refn|group=note|name=EIDunconfirmed}}
|
| align="right" | {{coord|30|26|40|S|115|46|16|E|name=Yallalie}}
|-
| [[Zerelia West]]
| [[Magnesia (regional unit)|Magnesia]]
|Greece
| 20
| {{sort|0.007|0.0070<br />(5000 BC)}}
|
| align="center" | <ref name="ZereliaRu">[http://labmpg.sscc.ru/a1360.html Zerelia East & West]</ref><ref name="zerelia" />
| rowspan="2" |
| align="right" | {{coord|39|09|48|N|22|42|32|E|name=Zerelia West}}
|-
| [[Zerelia East]]
| [[Magnesia (regional unit)|Magnesia]]
|Greece
| 10
| {{sort|0.007|0.0070<br />(5000 BC)}}
|
| align="center" | <ref name="ZereliaRu" /><ref name="zerelia">{{cite journal | bibcode = 2013SolED...5.1511D | title=The enigmatic Zerelia twin-lakes (Thessaly, Central Greece): two potential meteorite impact Craters | journal=Solid Earth Discussions | volume=5 | issue=2 | pages=1511–1573 | date=2013 | doi=10.5194/sed-5-1511-2013| last1=Dietrich | first1=V. J | last2=Lagios | first2=E | last3=Reusser | first3=E | last4=Sakkas | first4=V | last5=Gartzos | first5=E | last6=Kyriakopoulos | first6=K | s2cid=56034694 | doi-access=free }}</ref>
| align="right" | {{coord|39|09|43|N|22|42|51|E|name=Zerelia East}}
|-
|}

== Overview ==
Russia's [[Lake Cheko]] is thought by one research group to be the result of the famous [[Tunguska event]], although sediments in the lake have been dated back more than 5,000 years. There is highly speculative conjecture about the supposed [[Sirente crater|Sirente impact]] (c. 320 ± 90 AD) having caused the Roman emperor [[Constantine the Great|Constantine]]'s vision at [[Milvian Bridge]].<ref name="BBC-Whitehouse, D">{{cite web
  | last = Whitehouse 
  | first = David
  | title = Space impact 'saved Christianity'
  | work = BBC News
  | publisher = [[British Broadcasting Corporation]]
  | date = 2003-06-23
  | url = https://news.bbc.co.uk/2/hi/science/nature/3013146.stm
  | access-date = 2009-09-10 }}</ref>{{better source needed|date=April 2019}}

The [[Burckle crater]] and [[Umm al Binni lake|Umm al Binni structure]] are proposed to be behind the floods that affected [[Sumer]]ian civilization.<ref>Sandra Blakeslee (2006). [https://www.nytimes.com/2006/11/14/science/14WAVE.html Ancient Crash, Epic Wave]</ref><ref name=Master2002>Master, S. (2002) Umm al Binni lake, a possible Holocene impact structure in the marshes of southern Iraq. In: Leroy, S. and Stewart, I.S. (Eds.), Environmental Catastrophes and Recovery in the Holocene, Abstracts Volume, Brunel University, UK, 29 August – 2 September 2002, pp.&nbsp;56–57</ref> The Kachchh impact may have been witnessed by the [[Indus Valley Civilization|Harappan]] civilization and mentioned as a fireball in [[Sanskrit]] texts.<ref name="Kachchh">R. V. Karanth, P. Thakker, and M. Gadhavi 2006. [http://www.iisc.ernet.in/~currsci/oct102006/877.pdf A preliminary report on the possible impact crater of Kachchh] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20160701150024/http://www.iisc.ernet.in/~currsci/oct102006/877.pdf |date=2016-07-01 }}, Current Science, vol. 91, no. 7, October 2006</ref>

Shortly after the Hiawatha Crater was discovered, researchers suggested that the impact could have occurred as late as ~12,800 years ago, leading some to associate it with the controversial [[Younger Dryas impact hypothesis]] (YDIH).<ref name="InitialStudy">{{cite journal|first=Kurt H. |last=Kjær |display-authors=|doi=10.1126/sciadv.aar8173|pmid=30443592 |pmc=6235527 |title=A large impact crater beneath Hiawatha Glacier in northwest Greenland|journal=Science Advances |volume=4 |issue=11 |article-number=eaar8173 |date=November 2018 |bibcode=2018SciA....4.8173K }}</ref> James Kennett, a leading advocate of the [[YDIH]] said, "I'd unequivocally predict that this crater is the same age as the Younger Dryas."<ref name="Voosen2">{{Cite web |last=Voosen |first=Paul |name-list-style=vanc |date=14 November 2018 |title=Massive crater under Greenland's ice points to climate-altering impact in the time of humans |url=https://www.science.org/content/article/massive-crater-under-greenland-s-ice-points-climate-altering-impact-time-humans |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20190113194450/https://www.sciencemag.org/news/2018/11/massive-crater-under-greenland-s-ice-points-climate-altering-impact-time-humans |archive-date=13 January 2019 |access-date=13 January 2019 |website=Sciencemag.org |publisher=[[Science (journal)|Science]]}}</ref>

These claims were criticised by other scholars. According to impact physicist [[Mark Boslough]] writing for ''[[Skeptical Inquirer]]'' the first reports of the impact released by science journalist Paul Voosen focused on this being a young crater which according to Boslough "set the tone for virtually all the media reporting to follow". Boslough argued, based on evidence and statistical probability, that once the crater has been drilled and researched "it will turn out to be much older." He complained that this important discovery "was tainted by connections to a widely discredited hypothesis and speculations that did not make it through peer review".<ref name="Voosen2"/><ref>{{Cite journal |last=Boslough |first=Mark |author-link=Mark Boslough |name-list-style=vanc |date=March 2019<!-- March/April 2019 --> |title=Crater Discovery Story Flawed by Premature Link to Speculative Impact Hypothesis |journal=[[Skeptical Inquirer]] |volume=43 |issue=2 |pages=6–7}}</ref> The [[YDIH]] has since been refuted comprehensively by a team of earth scientists and impact experts.<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Holliday |first1=Vance T. |last2=Daulton |first2=Tyrone L. |last3=Bartlein |first3=Patrick J. |last4=Boslough |first4=Mark B. |last5=Breslawski |first5=Ryan P. |last6=Fisher |first6=Abigail E. |last7=Jorgeson |first7=Ian A. |last8=Scott |first8=Andrew C. |last9=Koeberl |first9=Christian |last10=Marlon |first10=Jennifer |last11=Severinghaus |first11=Jeffrey |last12=Petaev |first12=Michail I. |last13=Claeys |first13=Philippe |date=2023-07-26 |title=Comprehensive refutation of the Younger Dryas Impact Hypothesis (YDIH) |journal=Earth-Science Reviews |volume=247 |language=en |article-number=104502 |doi=10.1016/j.earscirev.2023.104502|s2cid=260218223 |doi-access=free |bibcode=2023ESRv..24704502H }}</ref>

A 2022 study using [[argon–argon dating|Argon–Argon dating]] of shocked [[Zircon|zircon crystals]] in [[Impactite|impact melt rocks]] found [[Outwash plain|outwash]] less than 10&nbsp;km downstream of the glacier pushed the estimate back to around 57.99 ± 0.54 million years ago, during the late [[Paleocene]].<ref>{{cite web |last1=Copenhagen |first1=University of |title=Giant impact crater in Greenland occurred a few million years after dinosaurs went extinct |url=https://phys.org/news/2022-03-giant-impact-crater-greenland-million.html |website=phys.org |archive-url= https://web.archive.org/web/20220310020001/https://phys.org/news/2022-03-giant-impact-crater-greenland-million.html |archive-date=10 March 2022 |language=en |date=9 March 2022 |url-status=live}}</ref><ref name="KennyOthers2022">{{cite journal |last1=Kenny |first1=Gavin G. |last2=Hyde |first2=William R. |last3=Storey |first3=Michael |last4=Garde |first4=Adam A. |last5=Whitehouse |first5=Martin J. |last6=Beck |first6=Pierre |last7=Johansson |first7=Leif |last8=Søndergaard |first8=Anne Sofie |last9=Bjørk |first9=Anders A. |last10=MacGregor |first10=Joseph A. |last11=Khan |first11=Shfaqat A. |last12=Mouginot |first12=Jérémie |last13=Johnson |first13=Brandon C. |last14=Silber |first14=Elizabeth A. |last15=Wielandt |first15=Daniel K. P. |last16=Kjær |first16=Kurt H. |last17=Larsen |first17=Nicolaj K. |title=A Late Paleocene age for Greenland's Hiawatha impact structure |journal=Science Advances |date=11 March 2022 |volume=8 |issue=10 |article-number=eabm2434 |doi=10.1126/sciadv.abm2434|pmid=35263140|pmc=8906741 |bibcode=2022SciA....8M2434K }}</ref> Confirmation would require drilling almost {{cvt|1|km|ft|spell=in}} through the ice sheet above the crater to obtain a sample of dateable, solidified impact melt from the crater.

The age of the [[Bloody Creek crater]]<ref name="StevensOthers2008">{{cite journal |last1=Stevens |first1=G |first2=I |last2=Spooner |first3=J |last3=Morrow |first4=P |last4=Pufahl |first5=R |last5=Raeside |first6=RAF |last6=Grieve |first7=CR |last7=Stanley |first8=SM |last8=Barr |first9=D |last9=McMullin |year=2008 |title=Physical evidence of a late-glacial (Younger Dryas?) impact event in southwestern Nova Scotia |journal=Atlantic Geology |volume=44 |page=42}}</ref> is uncertain.

As the trend in the Earth Impact Database for about 26 confirmed craters younger than a million years old shows that almost all are less than {{cvt|2|km|spell=in||||}} in diameter (except the {{cvt|3|km|spell=in||||}} Agoudal and {{cvt|4|km|spell=in||||}} [[Rio Cuarto craters|Rio Cuarto]]), the suggestion that two large craters, [[Mahuika crater|Mahuika]] ({{cvt|20|km|||||}}) and [[Burckle crater|Burckle]] ({{cvt|30|km|||||}}), formed only within the last few millennia has been met with skepticism.<ref>{{Cite journal|author=Goff, James|year=2010|title=Analysis of the Mahuika comet impact tsunami hypothesis|journal=[[Marine Geology (journal)|Marine Geology]]|volume=271|issue=3/4 |pages=292–296|doi=10.1016/j.margeo.2010.02.020|display-authors=etal|bibcode=2010MGeol.271..292G}}</ref><ref>{{cite journal |doi=10.1130/G25246A.1 |url=http://faculty.washington.edu/jbourgeo/BourgeoisWeiss2009final.pdf |title='Chevrons' are not mega-tsunami deposits – A sedimentologic assessment|year=2009|last1=Bourgeois|first1=Joanne|last2=Weiss|first2=Robert|journal=Geology|volume=37|issue=5|pages=403–406|bibcode=2009Geo....37..403B}}</ref><ref>{{cite journal|doi=10.1130/GSAT01801GW.1|title=Impacts, mega-tsunami, and other extraordinary claims|year=2008|last1=Pinter|first1=Nicholas|last2=Ishman|first2=Scott E.|journal=GSA Today|volume=18|issue=1 |page=37|doi-access=free|bibcode=2008GSAT...18a..37P }}</ref> However, the source of the young (less than a million years old) and enormous [[Australasian strewnfield]] (c. 790 [[kiloannum|ka]]) is suggested to be a crater about {{cvt|100|km|||||}} across somewhere in Indochina,<ref name=povenmire>Povenmire H., Liu W. and Xianlin I. (1999) [http://www.lpi.usra.edu/meetings/LPSC99/pdf/1072.pdf "Australasian tektites found in Guangxi Province, China"], 30th Annual Lunar and Planetary Science Conference, Houston, March 1999.</ref><ref name=glass1994>{{Cite journal |doi = 10.1029/94JE01866|title = Geographic variation in Australasian microtektite concentrations: Implications concerning the location and size of the source crater|year = 1994|last1 = Glass|first1 = B. P.|last2 = Pizzuto|first2 = J. E.|journal = Journal of Geophysical Research|volume = 99|issue = E9|page = 19075|bibcode = 1994JGR....9919075G}}</ref> with Hartung and Koeberl (1994) proposing the elongated {{cvt|100|x|35|km|||}} [[Tonlé Sap|Tonlé Sap lake]] in [[Cambodia]] (visible in the map at the side) as a suspect structure.<ref name=hartung>{{Cite journal | doi=10.1111/j.1945-5100.1994.tb00606.x| title=In search of the Australasian tektite source crater: The Tonle Sap hypothesis| year=1994| last1=Hartung| first1=Jack| last2=Koeberl| first2=Christian| journal=Meteoritics| volume=29| issue=3| pages=411–416| bibcode=1994Metic..29..411H}}</ref>

The [[Decorah crater]] has been conjectured as being part of the [[Ordovician meteor event]].<ref name=Vastag>{{cite news|last=Vastag|first=Brian|title=Crater found in Iowa points to asteroid break-up 470 million years ago|url=https://www.washingtonpost.com/national/health-science/crater-found-in-iowa-points-to-asteroid-break-up-470-million-years-ago/2013/02/18/545131f8-76d5-11e2-aa12-e6cf1d31106b_story.html?wprss=rss_national|access-date=19 February 2013|newspaper=[[Washington Post]]|date=18 February 2013}}</ref>{{better source needed|date=April 2019}}

Several twin impacts have been proposed, such as the [[Rubielos de la Cérida impact structure|Rubielos de la Cérida]] and [[Azuara impact structure|Azuara]] (30–40 Ma),<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Ernstson |first1=K. |last2=Claudin |first2=F. |last3=Schüssler |first3=U. |last4=Hradil |first4=K. |year=2002 |url=http://www.impact-structures.com/pdfall.pdf |title=The mid-Tertiary Azuara and Rubielos de la Cérida paired impact structures (Spain)|journal=Treb. Mus. Geol. Barcelona |volume=11 |pages=5–65}}</ref> Cerro Jarau and Piratininga (c. 117 Ma),<ref name=braz1/> and [[East Warburton Basin|Warburton East and West]] (300–360 Ma).<ref name=war>[http://www.cbc.ca/news/technology/world-s-largest-asteroid-impact-zone-found-in-australia-1.3006800 World's largest asteroid impact zone found in Australia]: Meteorite broke in two, leaving two craters each 200 km across. Mar 24, 2015</ref> However, adjacent craters may not necessarily have formed at the same time, as demonstrated by the case of the confirmed [[Clearwater Lakes|Clearwater East and West]] lakes.

Some confirmed impacts like [[Sudbury Basin|Sudbury]] or [[Chicxulub crater|Chicxulub]] are also sources of [[magnetic anomalies]]<ref>[http://geogratis.gc.ca/api/en/nrcan-rncan/ess-sst/b6c521eb-5155-522a-96cc-b8c9e5ed4b65.html Magnetic anomaly map, Sudbury, Ontario and Quebec]. Natural Resources Canada</ref> and/or [[gravity anomalies]]. The magnetic anomalies [[Bangui magnetic anomaly|Bangui]] and Jackpine Creek,<ref name=jackpine/> the gravity anomalies [[Wilkes Land crater]] and Falkland Islands,<ref name=Malvinas>{{cite journal |first1=M. |last1=Rocca |first2=J. |last2=Presser |year=2015 |url=https://www.researchgate.net/publication/283123311 |title=A possible new very large impact structure in Malvinas Islands |journal=Historia Natural, Tercera Series |volume=5 |issue=2}}</ref> and others have been considered as being of impact origin. Bangui apparently has been discredited,<ref name=master/><ref>L. Antoine, W. Reimold, and A. Tessema (1999) [http://www.lpi.usra.edu/meetings/metsoc99/pdf/5087.pdf The Bangui Magnetic Anomaly Revisited], 62nd Annual Meteoritical Society Meeting</ref> but appears again in a 2014 table of unconfirmed structures in Africa by Reimold and Koeberl.<ref name=Africa/>

Several anomalies in [[Williston Basin]] were identified by Swatzky in the 1970s as [[astrobleme]]s including [[Viewfield crater|Viewfield]], [[Red Wing crater|Red Wing Creek]], [[Eagle Butte crater|Eagle Butte]], [[Dumas magnetic anomaly|Dumas]], and [[Hartney anomaly|Hartney]], of which only the last two are unconfirmed.<ref name=dumas/>

The [[Eltanin impact]] has been confirmed (via an [[iridium anomaly]] and meteoritic material from ocean cores) but, as it fell into the [[Pacific Ocean]], apparently no crater was formed. The age of [[Silverpit crater|Silverpit]] and the confirmed [[Boltysh crater]] (65.17 ± 0.64 Ma), as well as their [[latitude]], has led to the speculative hypothesis that there may have been several impacts during the KT boundary.<ref>Howard Falcon-Lang (2010). [https://www.bbc.co.uk/news/science-environment-11112417 Double space strike 'caused dinosaur extinction'], BBC News</ref><ref>{{cite journal | last1 = Jolley | first1 = D. | last2 = Gilmour | first2 = I. | last3 = Gurov | first3 = E. | last4 = Kelley | first4 = S. | last5 = Watson | first5 = J. | year = 2010 | title = Two large meteorite impacts at the Cretaceous-Paleogene boundary | journal = Geology | volume = 38 | issue = 9| pages = 835–838 | doi = 10.1130/G31034.1 | bibcode = 2010Geo....38..835J | s2cid = 120172960 | url = http://oro.open.ac.uk/22382/1/Jolley_et_al_2010.pdf }}</ref> Of the five [[ocean]]s in descending order by area, namely the [[Pacific Ocean|Pacific]], [[Atlantic Ocean|Atlantic]], [[Indian Ocean|Indian]], [[Southern Ocean|Antarctic]], and [[Arctic Ocean|Arctic]], only the smallest (the Arctic) does not yet have a proposed unconfirmed impact crater.

Craters larger than {{convert|100|km|mi}} in the [[Phanerozoic]] (after 541 Ma) are notable for their size as well as for the possible coeval events associated with them especially the major [[extinction event]]s.

For example, the Ishim impact structure<ref name=dachille/> is conjectured to be bounded by the late [[Ordovician]]-early [[Silurian]] (c. 445 ± 5 Ma),<ref name=zeylik1/> the two [[East Warburton Basin|Warburton basins]] have been linked to the [[Late Devonian extinction]] (c. 360 Ma),<ref name=glickson1/> both [[Bedout]] and the [[Wilkes Land crater]] have been associated with the severe [[Permian–Triassic extinction event]] (c. 252 Ma),<ref name="Becker2006"/><ref name="big bang"/> [[Manicouagan impact structure|Manicouagan]] (c. 215 Ma) was once thought to be connected to the [[Triassic–Jurassic extinction event]] (c. 201 Ma)<ref name=Hodych/> but more recent dating has made it unlikely, while the consensus is the [[Chicxulub impact]] caused the one for [[Cretaceous–Paleogene extinction event|Cretaceous–Paleogene]] (c. 66 Ma).

However, other extinction theories employ coeval periods of [[Flood basalt#List of flood basalts|massive volcanism]] such as the [[Siberian Traps]] (Permian-Triassic) and [[Deccan Traps]] (Cretaceous-Paleogene).

== Undiscovered but inferred ==
[[File:Australasian strewnfield.jpg|thumb|250px|alt=An approximate map of the strewnfield.|Australasian [[strewnfield]]. Shaded areas represent [[tektite]] finds.]]
There is geological evidence for impact events having taken place on Earth on certain specific occasions, which should have formed craters, but for which no impact craters have been found. In some cases this is because of erosion and Earth's crust having been recycled through plate tectonics, in others likely because exploration of the Earth's surface is incomplete, or because no actual crater was formed because the impacting object exploded as a cosmic air burst. Typically the ages are already known and the diameters can be estimated.

{| class="wikitable sortable"
! Parent crater of
! Expected crater diameter
! Age
! Notes
|-
| [[Pica glass]]
| Unknown
| 12 [[Year#SI prefix multipliers|ka]]
| align=center | <ref>{{cite journal |last1=Schultz |first1=P. H. |last2=Harris |first2=R. S. |last3=Perroud |first3=S. |last4=Blanco |first4=N.|last5=Tomlinson |first5=S.|title=Widespread glasses generated by cometary fireballs during the late Pleistocene in the Atacama Desert, Chile |journal=Geology |volume=50 |page=205 |date=2022 |issue=2 |doi=10.1130/G49426.1 |bibcode=2022Geo....50..205S |s2cid=242062030 |doi-access=free }}</ref>
|-
| [[Libyan desert glass]]
| Unknown
| 29 [[Year#SI prefix multipliers|Ma]]
| align=center | <ref name="CavosieOthers2019a">{{cite journal|last1=Cavosie|first1=A.J.
|last2=Koeberl|first2=C.|title=Overestimation of threat from 100 Mt–class airbursts? High-pressure evidence from zircon in Libyan Desert Glass|journal=Geology|volume=47|issue=7|pages=609–612|date=2019|doi=10.1130/G45974.1
|bibcode=2019Geo....47..609C
|s2cid=155125330
}}</ref><ref name="KoeberlOthers2019a">{{cite journal|last1=Koeberl|first1=C. 
|last2=Ferrière|first2=L.|title=Libyan Desert Glass area in western Egypt: Shocked quartz in bedrock points to a possible deeply eroded impact structure in the region|journal=Meteoritics & Planetary Science|volume=54|issue=10|pages=2398–2408|date=2019|doi=10.1111/maps.13250 
|bibcode=2019M&PS...54.2398K 
|s2cid=134219301 
|doi-access=free}}</ref><ref name="SighinolfiOthers2020a">{{cite journal|last1=Sighinolfi|first1=G.P.|last2=Lugli|first2=F.|last3=Piccione|first3=F.
|last4=Michele|first4=V.D. |last5=Cipriani|first5=A.|title=Terrestrial target and melting site of Libyan Desert Glass: New evidence from trace elements and Sr isotopes|journal=Meteoritics & Planetary Science|volume=55|issue=8|pages=1865–1883|date=2020|doi=10.1111/maps.13550 |bibcode=2020M&PS...55.1865S |hdl=11380/1227258 |s2cid=225333357 |hdl-access=free}}</ref><ref name="SvestovOthers2020a">{{cite journal|last1=Sestov|first1=V.|last2=Shuvalov|first2= V.|last3=Kosarev|first3=I.|title=Formation of Libyan Desert Glass: Numerical simulations of melting of silica due to radiation from near-surface airbursts|journal=Meteoritics & Planetary Science|volume=55|issue=4|pages=895–910|date=2020|doi=10.1111/maps.13470 |bibcode=2020M&PS...55..895S |s2cid=216264770 }}</ref>
|-
| [[Dakhla Oasis|Dakhleh]] glass
| 0.4&nbsp;km
| 150 [[Year#SI prefix multipliers|ka]]
| align=center | <ref>{{cite journal |last1=Haldemann |first1=A. F. C. |last2=Kleindienst |first2=M. R. |last3=Churcher |first3=C. S. |last4=Smith |first4=J. R. |last5=Schwarcz |first5=H. P. |last6=Markham |first6=K. |last7=Osinski |first7=G. |title=Mapping Impact Modified Sediments: Subtle Remote-Sensing Signatures of the Dakhleh Oasis Catastrophic Event, Western Desert, Egypt |journal=Bulletin of the American Astronomical Society |volume=37 |page=648 |date=August 2005 |bibcode=2005DPS....37.1703H }}</ref><ref>G. Osinski, A. Haldemann, et al. (2007). [http://www.lpi.usra.edu/meetings/lpsc2007/pdf/1346.pdf Impact Glass At The Dakhleh Oasis, Egypt: Evidence For A Cratering Event Or Large Aerial Burst?], Lunar and Planetary Science XXXVIII</ref>
|-
| [[Argentinian impact glasses]]
| Unknown 
| 6, 114, and 445 [[Year#SI prefix multipliers|ka]];<br />5.3 and 9.2 [[Year#SI prefix multipliers|Ma]]
| align=center | <ref>{{cite journal |url=https://www.researchgate.net/publication/11367157 |doi=10.1126/science.1068345|title=A Possible Tektite Strewn Field in the Argentinian Pampa |year=2002|last1=Bland|first1=P. A.|last2=De Souza Filho|first2=C. R.|last3=Jull|first3=A. J. |last4=Kelley|first4=S. P.|last5=Hough|first5=R. M.|last6=Artemieva|first6=N. A.|author6-link=Natalia Artemieva|last7=Pierazzo|first7=E. |last8=Coniglio|first8=J.|last9=Pinotti|first9=L.|last10=Evers|first10=V. |last11=Kearsley|first11=A. T. |journal=Science|volume=296|issue=5570|pages=1109–1111|pmid=12004127|bibcode=2002Sci...296.1109B |s2cid=41279356}}</ref><ref name="SchultzOthers2004a">{{Cite journal|last1=Schulz|first1=P.H.|last2=Zárate|first2=M.|last3=Hames|first3=B.|last4=Koeberl|first4=C.|last5=Bunch|first5=T.|last6=Storzer|first6=D.|last7=Renne|first7=P.|last8= Wittke|first8=J.
|title=The Quaternary impact record from the Pampas, Argentina|journal=Earth and Planetary Science Letters|volume=219|issue=3–4|pages=221–238|doi= 10.1016/S0012-821X(04)00010-X|year=2004|bibcode=2004E&PSL.219..221S }}</ref><ref name="SchultzOthers2006a">{{Cite journal |last1=Schulz |first1=P.H. |last2=Zárate |first2=M. |last3=Hames |first3=B. |last4=Harris |first4=R.S. |last5=Bunch |first5=T. |last6=Koeberl |first6=C. |last7=Renne |first7=P. |last8=Wittke |first8=J. |year=2006 |title=The record of Miocene impacts in the Argentine Pampas |url=https://ri.conicet.gov.ar/handle/11336/81867 |journal=Meteoritics & Planetary Science |volume=41 |issue=5 |pages=749–771|doi=10.1111/j.1945-5100.2006.tb00990.x |bibcode=2006M&PS...41..749S |hdl=11336/81867 |s2cid=7590495 |hdl-access=free }}</ref>
|-
| [[Australasian Strewn Field|Australasian tektites]]
| 32–114&nbsp;km 
| 780 ka
| align=center | <ref name=glass1994/>
|-
| [[Central American tektites]]
| 14&nbsp;km 
| 820 ka
| align=center | <ref name="PovenmireOthers2012a">{{Cite journal|last1=Povenmire|first1=H.|last2=Burrer|first2=B.|last3=Cornec|first3=J.H.|last4=Harris|first4=R.S.|title=The New Central American Tektite Strewn Field Update|url=http://www.lpi.usra.edu/meetings/lpsc2012/pdf/1260.pdf|journal=43rd Lunar and Planetary Science Conference, Houston, Texas. Abstract No. 1260|volume= |issue= |pages= |doi= |year=2012}}</ref><ref name="SchwarzOthers2016a">{{Cite journal|last1=Schwarz|first1=W.H.|last2=Trieloff|first2=M.|last3=Bollinger|first3=K.|last4= Gantert|first4=N.|last5=Fernandes|first5=V.A.|last6=Meyer|first6=H.P.|last7=Povenmire|first7=H.|last8=Jessberger|first8=E.K.|last9=Guglielmino|first9=M.|last10=Koeberl|first10=C.|title=Coeval ages of Australasian, Central American and Western Canadian tektites reveal multiple impacts 790 ka ago|journal=Geochimica et Cosmochimica Acta|volume=178|issue= |pages=307–319|doi= 10.1016/j.gca.2015.12.037|year=2016|bibcode=2016GeCoA.178..307S }}</ref><ref name="KoeberlOthers2022a">{{Cite journal|last1=Koeberl|first1=C.|last2=Glass|first2=B.P.|last3=Schulz|first3=T.|last4=Wegner|first4=W.|last5=Giuli|first5=G.|last6=Cicconi|first6=M.R.|last7=Trapananti|first7=A.|last8= Stabile|first8=P.|last9=Cestelli-Guidi|first9=M.|last10=Park|first10=J.|last11=Herzog|first11=G.F.|title=Tektite glasses from Belize, Central America: Petrography, geochemistry, and search for a possible meteoritic component|journal=Geochimica et Cosmochimica Acta|volume=325|issue= |pages=232–257|doi= 10.1016/j.gca.2022.02.021|year=2022|bibcode=2022GeCoA.325..232K |s2cid=247063391 |doi-access=free|hdl=11581/470415|hdl-access=free}}</ref>
|-
| [[Skye ejecta deposits]]
| Unknown
| 60 [[Year#SI prefix multipliers|Ma]]
| align=center | <ref>{{Cite journal|last1=Drake|first1=Simon M.|last2=Beard|first2=Andrew D.|last3=Jones |first3=Adrian P.|last4=Brown|first4=David J.|last5=Fortes|first5=A. Dominic|last6=Millar|first6=Ian L. |last7=Carter|first7=Andrew|last8=Baca|first8=Jergus|last9=Downes|first9=Hilary|title=Discovery of a meteoritic ejecta layer containing unmelted impactor fragments at the base of Paleocene lavas, Isle of Skye, Scotland|journal=Geology|volume=46|issue=2|page=171|doi=10.1130/g39452.1|year=2017|doi-access=free |bibcode=2018Geo....46..171D}}</ref>
|-
| [[Stac Fada Member]]
| 40&nbsp;km 
| 1.2 [[Year#SI prefix multipliers|Ga]]
| align=center | <ref name="Simms">{{cite journal|last1=Simms|first1=Michael J.|title=The Stac Fada impact ejecta deposit and the Lairg Gravity Low: evidence for a buried Precambrian impact crater in Scotland? |journal=Proceedings of the Geologists' Association|date=December 2015|volume=126|issue=6|pages=742–761|doi=10.1016/j.pgeola.2015.08.010|bibcode=2015PrGA..126..742S |url=https://www.researchgate.net/publication/282570971|access-date=5 April 2017}}</ref><ref name="KennyOthers2019a">{{cite journal|last1=Kenny|first1=G.G.|last2=O'Sullivan|first2=G.J.|last3=Alexander|first3=S.|last4=Simms|first4=M.J.|last5=Chew|first5=D.M.|last6=Kamber|first6=B.S.|title=On the track of a Scottish impact structure: a detrital zircon and apatite provenance study of the Stac Fada Member and wider Stoer Group, NW Scotland|journal=Geological Magazine|volume=156|issue=11|pages=1863–1876|date=2019|doi= 10.1017/S0016756819000220|bibcode=2019GeoM..156.1863K |s2cid=150192833 |url=https://eprints.qut.edu.au/197844/8/on_the_track_of_a_scottish_impact_structure_a_detrital_zircon_and_apatite_provenance_study_of_the_stac_fada_member_and_wider_stoer_group_nw_scotland.pdf }}</ref><ref name="OsinskiOthers2020a">{{cite journal|last1=Osinski|first1=G.R.|last2=Ferrière|first2=L.|last3=Hill|first3=P.J.A.|last4=Pave|first4=A.R.|last5=Preston|first5=L.J.|last6=Singleton|first6=A.|last7=Pickersgill|first7=A.E.|title=The Mesoproterozoic Stac Fada Member, NW Scotland: an impact origin confirmed but refined|journal=Journal of the Geological Society|volume=178|issue=9|page=no. jgs2020-056|date=2014|doi= }}</ref>
|-
| [[Kaapvaal craton#Barberton greenstone belt|Barberton Greenstone Belt spherules]]
| 500&nbsp;km
| 3.2 Ga
| align=center | <ref name="SleepOthers2004a">{{cite journal|last1=Sleep |first1=N.H. |last2=Lowe |first2=D.R. |title=Physics of crustal fracturing and chert dike formation triggered by asteroid impact, ~3.26 Ga, Barberton greenstone belt, South Africa |journal= Geochemistry, Geophysics, Geosystems |volume=159 |pages=1045–1070 |date=2004 |issue=4}}</ref><ref name="LoweOthers2014a">{{cite journal|last1=Lowe |first1=D.R. |last2= Byerly |first2=G.R. |last3=Kyte |first3=F.T. |title=Recently discovered 3.42–3.23 Ga impact layers, Barberton Belt, South Africa: 3.8 Ga detrital zircons, Archean impact history, and tectonic implications |journal=Geology |volume=42 |pages=747–750  |date=2014 |issue=9|doi=10.1130/G35743.1 |bibcode=2014Geo....42..747L }}</ref>
|-
| [[North Pole Crater|Marble Bar impact spherules]]
| "hundreds of kilometers"
| 3.4 Ga
| align=center | <ref name="GliksonOther2016a">{{cite journal|last1=Glikson |first1=A. |last2=Hickman |first2=A. |last3=Evans |first3=N.J. |last4=Kirkland |first4=C.L. |last5=Park |first5=J.W. |last6=Rapp |first6=RS. |last7=Romanon |first7=S. |title=A new ~3.46 Ga asteroid impact ejecta unit at Marble Bar, Pilbara Craton, Western Australia: A petrological, microprobe and laser ablation ICPMS study |journal=Precambrian Research |volume=279 |pages=103–122 |date=2016 |doi=10.1016/j.precamres.2016.04.003|bibcode=2016PreR..279..103G }}</ref>
|-
|[[Kaveri Crater]]
|120&nbsp;km
|800 to 550 Ma
|align=center|<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Subrahmanya |first1=K. R. |last2=Prakash Narasimha |first2=K. N. |date=October 2017 |title=Kaveri crater – An impact structure in the Precambrian terrain of southern India |url=http://link.springer.com/10.1007/s12594-017-0733-5 |journal=Journal of the Geological Society of India |language=en |volume=90 |issue=4 |pages=387–395 |doi=10.1007/s12594-017-0733-5 |bibcode=2017JGSI...90..387S |issn=0016-7622 |s2cid=134717819|url-access=subscription }}</ref>
|}

== Mistaken identity ==
Some geological processes can result in circular or near-circular features that may be mistaken for impact craters. Some examples are [[calderas]], [[maar]]s, [[sinkholes]], [[glacial cirque]]s, [[intrusive rock|igneous intrusions]], [[ring dike]]s, [[salt domes]], [[dome (geology)|geologic domes]], [[ventifacts]], [[tuff ring]]s, [[forest ring]]s, and others. Conversely, an impact crater may originally be thought as one of these geological features, like [[Meteor Crater]] (as a [[maar]]) or [[Upheaval Dome]] (as a salt dome).

The presence of [[shock metamorphism]] and [[shatter cones]] are important criteria in favor of an impact interpretation, though massive landslides (such as the [[Sturzstrom|Köfels landslide]] of 7800 BC which was once thought to be impact-related) may produce shock-like fused rocks called "frictionite".<ref>{{Cite journal |author=Weidinger JT, Korup O |year=2008 |title=Frictionite as evidence for a large Late Quaternary rockslide near Kanchenjunga, Sikkim Himalayas, India – Implications for extreme events in mountain relief destruction |journal=Geomorphology |volume=103 |number=1 |pages=57–65 |doi=10.1016/j.geomorph.2007.10.021|bibcode=2009Geomo.103...57W }}</ref>
<gallery mode="packed" heights="120" style="text-align:left">
File:Crater lake oregon.jpg|[[Crater Lake]], Oregon (a ''[[caldera]]'')
File:Maare.jpg|Three ''[[maar]]s'' in Germany
File:Great Blue Hole.jpg|[[Great Blue Hole]], Belize (a ''[[sinkhole]]'')
File:2013-09-09 12 34 02 View of the Verdi Lake from Verdi Peaks.jpg|[[Verdi Lake (Nevada)|Verdi Lake]] (a ''[[glacial cirque]]'')
File:Kondyor-view.jpg|[[Kondyor Massif]] or Konder (an ''[[intrusive rock|igneous intrusion]]'')<ref>Mika McKinnon (2015). [http://space.gizmodo.com/this-is-not-a-crater-so-what-is-it-1677228983 This Is Not A Crater, So What Is It?] at space.gizmodo.com</ref>
File:Melville Island salt diapir.png|''[[Salt domes]]'' on [[Melville Island (Northwest Territories and Nunavut)|Melville Island]]
File:ASTER Richat.jpg|The [[Richat Structure|Richat structure]] (a ''[[dome (geology)|geologic dome)]]''<ref>{{cite journal | last1 = Dietz | first1 = R.S. | last2 = Fudali | first2 = R. | last3 = Cassidy | first3 = W. | year = 1969 | title = Richat and Semsiyat Domes (Mauritania): Not Astroblemes | url = http://gsabulletin.gsapubs.org/content/80/7/1367.short | journal = Geological Society of America | volume = 80 | issue = 7| pages = 1367–1372 | doi = 10.1130/0016-7606(1969)80[1367:rasdmn]2.0.co;2 | bibcode = 1969GSAB...80.1367D }}</ref>
File:Arkenu craters.jpg|The [[Arkenu structures]] (a pair of ''[[ring dike]]s)''
File:ISS-47 Brukkaros Mountain, Namibia.jpg|[[Brukkaros Mountain]] (a ''[[tuff ring]])''
File:Boreal Forest Ring.jpg|A ''[[forest ring]]'' in [[Ontario]]
</gallery>

== See also ==
* [[Earth Impact Database]]
* [[Gliese 710]] – example of gravitational perturbations (in mega-year scale) of the Solar System
* [[Impact Field Studies Group]]
* [[Impact crater]]s
* [[Impact event]]s
* [[List of impact craters on Earth]]
* [[Meteor air burst]]
* [[Tollmann's bolide hypothesis]]
* ''[[Traces of Catastrophe]]'', 1998 book from [[Lunar and Planetary Institute]] – comprehensive reference on impact crater science

=== References ===
{{reflist|30em|refs=
<ref name=dachille>{{Cite journal|title=Frequency of the formation of large terrestrial impact craters|journal=Meteoritics|volume=11|page=270|author=Frank Dachille (1976).|bibcode=1976Metic..11..270D|year=1976}}</ref>
<ref name=zeylik1>Zeylik B. S.; Seytmuratova E. Yu, 1974: A meteorite-impact structure in central Kazakhstan and its magmatic-ore controlling role. Doklady Akademii Nauk SSSR: 1, pp. 167–170</ref>
<ref name=glickson1>Stephen Luntz (2013). [http://www.australasianscience.com.au/article/issue-may-2013/huge-asteroid-impact-identified.html Huge Asteroid Impact Identified]. Australasian Science</ref>
<ref name="Becker2006">Becker L., Shukolyukov A., Macassic C., Lugmair G. & Poreda R. 2006. Extraterrestrial Chromium at the Graphite Peak P/Tr boundary and in the Bedout Impact Melt Breccia. Lunar and Planetary Science XXXVII (2006), abstract # 2321.[http://www.lpi.usra.edu/meetings/lpsc2006/pdf/2321.pdf PDF]</ref>
<ref name="big bang">{{cite web |last=Gorder |first=Pam Frost |date=June 1, 2006 |title=Big Bang in Antarctica&nbsp;– Killer Crater Found Under Ice |publisher=Ohio State University Research News |url=http://researchnews.osu.edu/archive/erthboom.htm |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20160306140004/http://researchnews.osu.edu/archive/erthboom.htm |archive-date=March 6, 2016 }}</ref>
<ref name=Hodych>{{cite journal|first=J.P.|last=Hodych|author2=G.R.Dunning|title=Did the Manicouagan impact trigger end-of-Triassic mass extinction?|journal=Geology|volume=20|issue=1|date=1992|pages=51.54|doi=10.1130/0091-7613(1992)020<0051:DTMITE>2.3.CO;2|bibcode = 1992Geo....20...51H }}</ref>
}}

== External links ==
* [https://www.unb.ca/passc/ImpactDatabase/ Earth Impact Database] – List of confirmed earth impact sites at the Planetary and Space Science Centre, [[University of New Brunswick]]
* [https://www.datapages.com/gis-map-publishing-program/gis-open-files/geothematic/david-rajmon-global-impact-crater-gis-project David Rajmon Global Impact Crater GIS Project] (formerly either [https://web.archive.org/web/20140319132901/http://impacts.rajmon.cz/ Impact Databas] or [https://www.researchgate.net/profile/David-Rajmon/publication/252892732_Suspected_Earth_Impact_Sites/links/5639dea808ae4624b76051fe/Suspected-Earth-Impact-Sites.pdfhttps://www.researchgate.net/profile/David-Rajmon/publication/252892732_Suspected_Earth_Impact_Sites/links/5639dea808ae4624b76051fe/Suspected-Earth-Impact-Sites.pdf Suspected Earth Impact Sites list]) maintained by David Rajmon for [https://web.archive.org/web/20140816123552/http://web.eps.utk.edu/~faculty/ifsg.htm Impact Field Studies Group], US
